figures.caption: Figure 4: Visualization of the evaluation environments. (a) Multi-Destination Spread. (b) Counter Circuit and (c) Coordination Ring in Overcooked v1.
Figure B.2: Overcooked v1 layouts. (a) Forced Coordination. (b) Cramped Room. (c) Coordination Ring. (d) Counter Circuit. (e) Asymmetric Advantages.
Figure D.2: Seed-wise XP heatmaps on Overcooked v1.

Figure 1: Illustration of State-Blocked Coordination in Multi-Destination Spread. Top: Independently trained self-play policies may adopt incompatible conventions across seeds, causing failures in crossplay. Bottom: SBC creates virtual environmental variations through state blocking, inducing diverse suboptimal partner policies. Training the ego policy with these partners improves coordination with unseen partners following different conventions.
Table 2: Episode return on Overcooked v1. Values are reported as mean ± standard deviation, with bold indicating the best results.

Figure 5: Policy behavior analysis in Overcooked v1. Top: SP partners from different seeds exhibit diverse behaviors. Bottom: Blocking-aware partners induced by diverse penalty states cover this diversity, enabling robust coordination with unseen partners.
Figure D.3: Held-out XP heatmap on Multi-Destination Spread.
Figure B.1: Multi-Destination Spread. (a) Four agents spawn at the same central position. (b) Agents succeed by reaching four distinct goals.
Figure D.4: Held-out XP heatmaps on Overcooked v1. The bottom-right panel shows the mean across all five layouts.

Figure C.1: Web interface used in the real-human evaluation. (a) Pre-session instructions covering the goal, items, kitchen objects, and controls. (b) The gameplay screen, where the participant (blue, “You”) is paired with an ego agent (orange, “AI”) in a shared kitchen.

Figure 8: Human–AI behavioral analysis on Overcooked v1, averaged over five layouts (Wilcoxon signed-rank, p-value < 0.01). (a) Collisions per delivery (lower is better). (b) Participant-rated coordination fluency on a 7-point Likert scale (higher is better).
Figure 7: Human–AI evaluation on Overcooked v1 with scores normalized by each layout’s IPPO SP score. (a) Evaluation with human proxies. (b) Evaluation with real humans (p-value < 0.001).

Figure 3: Overview of the proposed SBC framework. Value-guided scheduling selects penalty states to construct SB-MDPs for learning blocking-aware policies, while the normal ego policy is trained in the original MDP through exposure to both self-play and blocking-aware partners.
Table C.1: Common IPPO hyperparameter settings shared across all methods within each environment.
